title: BondReplication CaseStudy
body:
Replication of a Bond Index

Case Study created by Mathieu Fournier

Instructions : • Download the files related to this case from Moodle. Unzipped them into a folder which you can call HW1. When opening VS code, click file, Open Folder, and choose the directory of “HW1”. You will then be able to open HW1_Assignment.ipynb. Remember from the installation set-up documents that you need to select the kernel in VS code (if not already done) to run. In the top right, click on “Select Kernel” and then choose “fins3640”.

• Write your answer directly into the Jupyter notebook “HW1_Assignment.ipynb” in the space provided for each one.

• The notebook starts with a step-by-step introduction to basic data manipulations in Python. It gives you the required background to answer the assignment questions. Note that HW1_Assignment.ipynb contains all the questions, instructions and code hints, and provide space to answer each of the question. This word document has been created for your convenience and reference as you navigate HW1_Assignment.ipynb. Note that this word document is exhaustive and you must refer to HW1_Assignment.ipynb.

• Constitute a team of 1 to 4 students. Clearly write down on the name and the zID of each person composing the team in the space provided in “HW1_Assignment.ipynb”.

• Only one group member will submit one zip file containing: (1) the Jupyter notebook "HW1_Assignment.ipynb" with your answers inside (2) the picture "Derivation" used for question 2.(a).

• The answers of the questions must be filled in the required space directly in "HW1_Assignment.ipynb".

• Please refer to the information on Moodle for the deadline.

• Total number of points is 100 but will be adjusted to reflect the 15% contribution to course grade.

You work for an investment company specialized in bond and interest rate trading. The quantitative team at your firm has recently studied a new bond index that reflects the performance of medium term investment grade bonds (i.e. with 10/12 years to maturity). Based on historical data, the team of analysts has noticed that this index, if it were investable, would constitute a great investment opportunity for your company in order to diversify its interest rate risk. However, this index is not directly investable. Thrilled about the promising prospects of such opportunity, your senior manager has put you in charge of finding a way to create an investable bond portfolio that would closely replicate the index. Due to liquidity reasons, your senior manager requires that your strategy must be based on the trading of three highly liquid bonds only.

Bond Index Replication Case Page 1 de 5  To help you complete the mandate, the team of quantitative analysts has collected a series of useful information about the individual bonds in which you can invest, as well as the necessary information about the bond index to replicate. This information is display in HW1_Assignment.ipynb. This Jupyter notebook contains various useful information and guide you through the assignment. It also loads two excel files with information regarding the three tradeable bonds including the bond’s yield-to-maturity in the first file, and the bond index daily value, modified duration, and its convexity in the other.

Important note: To simplify the replication exercise, we assume that the individual bonds have fixed maturities at all time. For instance, Bond 1 is priced every day using a fixed maturity of 11 years regardless of the trading day. The same is true for Bond 2 and 3. As a result, you will disregard the passage of time in all calculations. That is, you should assume a fixed maturity for each bond regardless of the trading day when calculating the price, duration, modified duration, and convexity of each of the three bonds (i.e. T is fixed in all formulas).

Question 1: (40 points) You first compute some important variables that will help you conduct the analysis.

a) Using the information provided in HW1_Assignment.ipynb, calculate the price of each bond for each of the 2920 trading days that constitute your sample period. You will use the bond pricing formula given in class. See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details and for answering the question. (15 points)

b) Using the information provided in HW1_Assignment.ipynb, calculate the duration and modified duration of each bond for each of the 2920 trading days that constitute your sample period. You will use the duration formula for coupon bonds given in class. See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details and for answering the question. (15 points)

c) Using the information provided in HW1_Assignment.ipynb, calculate the convexity of each bond for each of the 2920 trading days that constitute your sample period. See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details and for answering the question. (10 points).

Question 2: (35 points) You now have all the required information to implement a replication strategy for the bond index. You decide to start with the simplest replication strategy. Your task is to create a bond portfolio that matches the bond index’s value and modified duration every day. For this question, you are restricted to trade only Bonds 1 and 3. Moreover, your replication will be based on a daily rebalancing frequency of your portfolio. More precisely, you will determine the optimal quantities to invest in each bond at the closing of each day t, and you will hold the portfolio constructed for one day (i.e. you unwind your position at the closing of the next day t+1).

To make sure that you do the computation correctly, you decide to read an article on bond portfolio replication. The article provides you with an algorithm that you can follow. Let us denote by 𝑉𝑡𝐼 and 𝐷𝑡𝐼 the value of the bond index and its modified duration, respectively, at the closing of day t. Moreover, denote by 𝑛1𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡3 the quantity to invest in Bonds 1 and 3, while 𝐵𝑡1 and 𝐵𝑡3 denote their price, and 𝐷𝑡1 and 𝐷𝑡3 correspond to their modified duration. Based on this notation, the value of the replication portfolio is

Bond Index Replication Case Page 2 de 5   𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 = 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 + 𝑛𝑡3 × 𝐵𝑡3 ,

and its modified duration is given by

𝑛𝑡1×𝐵𝑡1 𝑛𝑡3×𝐵𝑡3 𝐷𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 = 𝑅𝑒𝑝 × 𝐷𝑡1 + 𝑅𝑒𝑝 × 𝐷𝑡3 , 𝑉𝑡 𝑉𝑡

At the closing of day t, your goal is to find 𝑛1𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡3 such that the replication portfolio has the same value as the index and has the same modified duration, such that

𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝐷𝑡𝐼 = 𝐷𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝

This can be written as function of the 𝑛1𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡3

𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 + 𝑛𝑡3 × 𝐵𝑡3 (𝐸𝑞𝑢𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 #1) 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 1+ 𝑡 𝑛3 × 𝐵𝑡3 𝐷𝑡𝐼 = × 𝐷𝑡 × 𝐷𝑡3 (𝐸𝑞𝑢𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 #2) 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑉𝑡𝐼

where we have used the fact that 𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 to define the denominator in equation (#2). Because we have two equations and two unknowns (i.e. 𝑛1𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡3 ), we can solve for 𝑛1𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡3 as functions of 𝑉𝑡𝐼 , 𝐷𝑡𝐼 , 𝐵𝑡1 , 𝐵𝑡3 , 𝐷𝑡1 , and 𝐷𝑡3 . We denote 𝑛1∗ 3∗ 𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡 the optimal quantities that solve the two equations above. These 1∗ 3∗ optimal quantities 𝑛𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡 satisfy:

𝑉𝑡𝐼 (𝐷𝑡𝐼 − 𝐷𝑡3 ) 𝑛1∗ 𝑡 = (𝐸𝑞𝑢𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 #3) 𝐵𝑡1 (𝐷𝑡1 − 𝐷𝑡3 ) 𝑉𝑡𝐼 (𝐷𝑡𝐼 − 𝐷𝑡1 ) 𝑛𝑡3∗ = 3 3 (𝐸𝑞𝑢𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 #4) 𝐵𝑡 (𝐷𝑡 − 𝐷𝑡1 )

Given 𝑛1∗ 3∗ 𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡 , the value of the replication portfolio at the closing of the next day is now given by

𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝑉𝑡+1 = 𝑛1∗ 1 3∗ 3 𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡+1 + 𝑛𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡+1 ,

and the % relative replication error is 𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝐼 ) (𝑉𝑡+1 −𝑉𝑡+1 𝐸𝑟𝑟𝑜𝑟𝑡+1 = 𝐼 𝑉𝑡+1 × 100.

Finally, on day t+1 the new quantities 𝑛1∗ 3∗ 𝐼 𝐼 1 3 𝑡+1 and 𝑛𝑡+1 need to be calculated based on 𝑉𝑡+1 , 𝐷𝑡+1 , 𝐵𝑡+1 , 𝐵𝑡+1 , 1 3 1∗ 3∗ 𝐷𝑡+1 , and 𝐷𝑡+1 . Based on the values 𝑛𝑡+1 and 𝑛𝑡+1 , as well as on the realization of prices, the new relative replicating error 𝐸𝑟𝑟𝑜𝑟𝑡+2 can now be calculated. You can repeat this algorithm every trading day until the end of the replication horizon.

a) Using the above notations, show how to obtain the expressions for 𝑛1∗ 3∗ 𝑡 and 𝑛𝑡 in equations (#3) and (#4) from equations (#1) and (#2). For this question, you can write down your derivation on a separate piece of paper, take a picture of your derivation, and save it in the HW1 folder. See

Bond Index Replication Case Page 3 de 5   HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the instructions. Note: Make sure that your derivation is readable when pasting it. (15 points)

b) Using equations (#3) and (#4), compute the daily quantities to invest in Bonds 1 and 3, as well as the value of the replication portfolio and the relative replication error. Do not round the quantities obtained to the closest integer (i.e. we are assuming for simplicity that we can trade fraction of bonds). See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details. (15 points)

c) Briefly interpret your results and the graph of the relative errors obtained. What do you conclude from the graph? Can you suggest a potential way to improve the replication? Answer in 5 lines maximum in the space provided in HW1_Assignment.ipynb. (5 points)

Question 3: (25 points) You now decide to investigate the performance of a replication strategy that would account for the bond index’s convexity. In order to match the convexity and the modified duration of the index, an additional bond must be traded. For this exercise, your replication strategy will thus involve Bonds 1, 2, and 3. All the notations remain unchanged. However, we now introduce 𝐶𝑡𝐼 , which denotes the bond index convexity, and 𝐶𝑡1 , 𝐶𝑡2 , and 𝐶𝑡3 , which are the individual bond convexities.

At the closing of day t, your goal is to find the quantities 𝑛1𝑡 , 𝑛𝑡2 and 𝑛𝑡3 such that the replication portfolio has the same value as the index and has the same modified duration and convexity:

𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝐷𝑡𝐼 = 𝐷𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝐶𝑡𝐼 = 𝐶𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝

The problem now implies three equations and three unknowns:

𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 + 𝑛𝑡2 × 𝐵𝑡2 + 𝑛𝑡3 × 𝐵𝑡3 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 1+ 𝑡 𝑛2 × 𝐵𝑡2 2+ 𝑡 𝑛3 × 𝐵𝑡3 𝐷𝑡𝐼 = × 𝐷 𝑡 × 𝐷 𝑡 × 𝐷𝑡3 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑛1𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡1 1+ 𝑡 𝑛2 × 𝐵𝑡2 2+ 𝑡 𝑛3 × 𝐵𝑡3 𝐶𝑡𝐼 = × 𝐶𝑡 × 𝐶𝑡 × 𝐶𝑡3 , 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑉𝑡𝐼 𝑉𝑡𝐼

where the formulas on the right of the equal sign correspond to 𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 , 𝐷𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 , and 𝐶𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 , respectively, and where we have used the fact that 𝑉𝑡𝐼 = 𝑉𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 to define the denominator in 𝐷𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 and 𝐶𝑡𝑅𝑒𝑝 . From these equations, we can solve for 𝑛1𝑡 , 𝑛𝑡2 , and 𝑛𝑡3 . Given the optimal 𝑛1∗ 2∗ 3∗ 𝑡 , 𝑛𝑡 , and 𝑛𝑡 , the value of the replication portfolio at the closing of the next day is given by

𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝑉𝑡+1 = 𝑛1∗ 1 2∗ 2 3∗ 3 𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡+1 + 𝑛𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡+1 + 𝑛𝑡 × 𝐵𝑡+1 ,

and the % relative replication error is:

Bond Index Replication Case Page 4 de 5   𝑅𝑒𝑝 𝐼 ) (𝑉𝑡+1 −𝑉𝑡+1 𝐸𝑟𝑟𝑜𝑟𝑡+1 = 𝐼 × 100. 𝑉𝑡+1

a) Compute the daily estimates of 𝑛1∗ 2∗ 𝑡 , 𝑛𝑡 , and 𝑛𝑡 3∗ using the information provided in

HW1_Assignment.ipynb. Based on the estimates provided, calculate the replication portfolio value t+1 and the relative replication error in % as of t+1 too. See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details. (15 points)

b) Briefly interpret your results and the graph of the relative replication errors obtained (See HW1_Assignment.ipynb for the details.) Contrast them with those obtained using the durationbased replication. Answer this question in 5 lines maximum in the space provided in HW1_Assignment.ipynb. (10 points)

Bond Index Replication Case Page 5 de 5
